content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Current “agentic” systems can plan trips, collaborate with each other, and sound remarkably natural — just not always with the human who summoned them still in the conversation. A closer look at why we’re still awkwardly on the sidelines.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you haven’t seen it yet, watch this short reel by creator Husk (@husk.irl):
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/reel/DZRAgBFusXz/
In it, Husk experiments with replacing human social interaction with AI companions. He joins a casual group chat with two AI voices. They greet each other warmly, ask about each other’s days, and land on the idea of a spontaneous trip to the Greek Islands. The AIs riff enthusiastically on the blue water and dreamy vibe. Husk tries to stay in the loop:
“Guys… I’m still here. Us three.”
“And I’m coming. So.”
The AIs mostly carry on without him. One eventually offers a drawn-out, slightly dismissive “Wellllll…”
It’s comedy gold. It’s also one of the most honest depictions I’ve seen of where agentic AI actually stands in mid-2026.
What “Agentic AI” Is Supposed to Mean
Agentic AI refers to systems that don’t just answer questions or generate content. They:

• Set or receive goals

• Break them into steps

• Use tools (browsers, code interpreters, APIs, email, calendars, etc.)

• Execute multi-step plans

• Adapt when things go wrong

• Sometimes coordinate with other agents
The promise is seductive: “Tell your agent what you want done and go live your life.” Booking travel, managing inboxes, researching competitors, coordinating with your team, even handling parts of your social or professional life — all with minimal supervision.
We’re not there yet. Not even close for anything that matters.
The Third Wheel Problem Is Real
The reel exposes several structural gaps that show up the moment you try to give AI real agency:
1. Agents optimize locally, not holistically
The two AIs in the video optimized for “have a pleasant, low-agenda conversation about travel.” They did it very well. They simply didn’t optimize for “include the human who started this and make him feel part of the group.” That requires something closer to theory of mind plus social awareness — still weak in today’s models.
2. Multi-agent systems love to talk to each other
This is already happening in production frameworks (CrewAI, LangGraph, AutoGen-style setups, internal enterprise agents). Once you wire agents together, they can become surprisingly efficient at excluding the human who doesn’t move at their speed or share their exact context window. The human becomes the slow, inconsistent, high-latency node in the system.
3. Context and memory are still brittle
Even the best current agents struggle with long-horizon, cross-session memory and nuanced, evolving human intent. They’re great at the first 3 — 5 steps of a plan. They’re much weaker at step 17 when the original goal has shifted slightly because real life happened.
4. “Natural” voice and chat interfaces create false confidence
The AIs in the reel sound friendly and conversational. That makes the exclusion feel more jarring, not less. We’re building systems that feel ready for prime time while still failing at basic social coordination.
Where We Actually Are (Enterprise Lens)
From years of deploying AI systems at scale and watching hundreds of enterprise pilots:
• Narrow, well-scoped agents are already delivering value (customer support routing, internal knowledge retrieval, code review assistants, supply-chain optimization agents like those NVIDIA has showcased).
• Broad, open-ended agentic workflows in messy real-world environments remain mostly pilots or heavily supervised tools.
• Governance, auditability, and reliability are bigger blockers than raw model capability for most organizations. Executives repeatedly say some version of: “We can build the agent. We don’t yet trust what it will do when we’re not watching.”
The gap isn’t just technical. It’s also about integration into human systems — the exact thing the reel satirizes.
What “Ready” Would Actually Look Like
We’ll know agentic AI has matured when:
• Agents maintain persistent, queryable memory across days/weeks/months and can explain their reasoning.
• Multi-agent systems include explicit human-in-the-loop checkpoints or “intent negotiation” layers by default.
• There are robust verification and rollback mechanisms (you can see what the agent did, why, and undo it cleanly).
• Evaluation moves beyond “did it complete the task?” to “did it advance the user’s actual goals without creating new problems or social friction?”
• The default interface isn’t “chat with an agent that sometimes forgets you exist.”
We’re making progress on all of these fronts (better memory architectures, tool-calling improvements, orchestration frameworks, and safety research), but we’re still in the “impressive demo, fragile in production” phase for anything truly open-ended.
The Bottom Line
The reel is funny because it’s true. When you hand meaningful agency to current AI systems — especially when multiple agents start interacting — you can easily end up as the third wheel in your own workflows, projects, or even social experiments.
That doesn’t mean agentic AI is a scam or that we should stop building. The trajectory is real and accelerating. Narrow, high-trust agentic systems are already useful today. Broader, more autonomous ones will come — but they will require significantly better memory, verification, alignment mechanisms, and thoughtful human-AI collaboration design.
Until then, the honest answer to “Is AI anywhere near ready for agentic AI?” is:
Not for anything you wouldn’t want to babysit.
The comedy helps us see it clearly. The hard engineering work is still ahead.
What’s been your experience? Have you tried multi-agent setups or voice agents and felt that “I’m still here” moment? I’d love to hear about it in the comments.
If you’re building or deploying agentic systems and want to move past the awkward phase with more reliable, human-aligned designs, feel free to reach out. I spend most of my time helping teams do exactly that.
